coredata.dc:title: Highly selective apo-arginase based method for sensitive enzymatic assay of manganese (II) and cobalt (II) ions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Stasyuk, Nataliya
Gayda, Galina
Zakalskiy, Andriy
Zakalska, Oksana
Errachid, Abdelhamid
Gonchar, Mykhailo
coredata.dc:description: A novel enzymatic method of manganese (II) and cobalt (II) ions assay, based on using apo-enzyme of Mn2+-dependent recombinant arginase I (arginase) and 2,3-butanedione monoxime (DMO) as a chemical reagent is proposed. The principle of the method is the evaluation of the activity of L-arginine-hydrolyzing of arginase holoenzyme after the specific binding of Mn2+ or Co2+ with apo-arginase. Urea, which is the product of enzymatic hydrolysis of L-arginine (Arg), reacts with DMO and the resulted compound is detected by both fluorometry and visual spectrophotometry. Thus, the content of metal ions in the tested samples can be determined by measuring the level of urea generated after enzymatic hydrolysis of Arg by reconstructed arginase holoenzyme in the presence of tested metal ions. The linearity range of the fluorometric apo-arginase-DMO method in the case of Mn2+ assay is from 4pM to 1.10nM with a limit of detection of 1pM Mn2+, whereas the linearity range of the present method in the case of Co2+ assay is from 8pM to 45nM with a limit of detection of 2.5pM Co2+. The proposed method being highly sensitive, selective, valid and low-cost, may be useful to monitor Mn2+ and Co2+ content in clinical laboratories, food industry and environmental control service.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Recombinant arginase I
Apoenzyme
Holoenzyme
Enzymatic analysis
Manganese (II) and cobalt (II) ions
L-arginine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calibration Graphs for Manganese (II) and Cobalt (II) Ions Assay
Metal Ions Assay in Real Samples
The Study of Optimal Conditions for Reconstitution of Arginase Holoenzyme and Enzymatic Hydrolysis of Arg
The Analytical Characteristics of Apo-Arginase-Based Method
Assay of Mn2+ and Co2+ in Real Wastewater Samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education and Science of Ukraine
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education and Science of Ukraine

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Academy of Sciences (NAS) of Ukraine (program “Sensors for Medical, Environmental, Industrial, and Technological Needs: Metrological Support and Trial Operation”, Project 13-2017), by the Ministry of Education and Science of Ukraine (project # 0116U004737 ), by NATO (Project CBP. NUKR.SFP 984173 ) and by fellowship of President of Ukraine for Young Scientists in 2017 (N. Stasyuk). We acknowledge the funding through the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and Programme entitled HEARTEN under grant agreement No 643694 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The principal scheme of apo-arginase based assay for Mn2+ or Co2+ ions using urea monitoring in reaction with DMO.

The dependence of the absorbance (D490) and fluorescence intensities (I, FIU) of final reaction product on the concentration of Mn2+ (A, B) and Co2+ (C, D) in final reaction mixture and linear calibration curves (inserts) for metal ions assay using apo-arginase-based method: DMO-S (A, C), DMO-F (B, D).

Selectivity of apo-arginase-DMO-F method to Mn2+ (A) and Co2+ (B) at the optimized conditions of holo-enzyme's reconstruction at pH9.1 (A) and 7.1 (B), followed by its enzymatic activity measurements.

Assay of Mn2+ (A, B) and Co2+ (C, D) ions by DMO-F method for two dilutions of the wastewater from gold (B, C) and copper (A, D) mines. [Mn2+] and [Co2+]−concentrations of added standards to the samples; C −calculated concentrations of Mn2+ and Co2+ ions in the analyzed samples.

Correlation between the results of Co2+ and Mn2+ contents (μM) estimated by enzymo-fluorometric method and atomic absorption method. Tested samples: Co2+ (1) and Mn2+ (3) from gold mine; Co2+ (4) and Mn2+ (2) from copper mine.
Characteristics of the proposed apo-arginase-DMO-based method.
Analysis of Mn2+ and Co2+ ions in real wastewater samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CV – coefficient of variation. Values are expressed as mean±SD. ⁎ – these results were obtained from curves, presented in Fig. 4.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results of the accuracy and convergence research of the fluorometric quantitative determination of Mn2+ and Co2+ ions in real wastewater samples.

row.entry: The criteria of accuracy and precision %

Critical values of
the one-sided confidence interval, х ≤3.2
Criterion of the systematic error insignificance δ≤Δprec/√18 if it is not satisfied 1), then 2) δ≤ maxδtot =2.3
The overall conclusion on the developed fluorometric method

head.ce:title: Highly selective apo-arginase based method for sensitive enzymatic assay of manganese (II) and cobalt (II) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Biotechnology, Institute of Cell Biology, NAS of Ukraine, Drahomanov Str. 14/16, Lviv 79005, Ukraine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Université de Lyon, Analytical Sciences Institute, UMR CNRS 5180, CNRS, Université de Lyon 1, ENS Lyon, 5 rue de la Doua, F-69100 Villeurbanne Cedex, France

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: ENS Lyon, 5 rue de la Doua

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel enzymatic method of manganese (II) and cobalt (II) ions assay, based on using apo-enzyme of Mn2+-dependent recombinant arginase I (arginase) and 2,3-butanedione monoxime (DMO) as a chemical reagent is proposed. The principle of the method is the evaluation of the activity of L-arginine-hydrolyzing of arginase holoenzyme after the specific binding of Mn2+ or Co2+ with apo-arginase. Urea, which is the product of enzymatic hydrolysis of L-arginine (Arg), reacts with DMO and the resulted compound is detected by both fluorometry and visual spectrophotometry. Thus, the content of metal ions in the tested samples can be determined by measuring the level of urea generated after enzymatic hydrolysis of Arg by reconstructed arginase holoenzyme in the presence of tested metal ions. The linearity range of the fluorometric apo-arginase-DMO method in the case of Mn2+ assay is from 4pM to 1.10nM with a limit of detection of 1pM Mn2+, whereas the linearity range of the present method in the case of Co2+ assay is from 8pM to 45nM with a limit of detection of 2.5pM Co2+. The proposed method being highly sensitive, selective, valid and low-cost, may be useful to monitor Mn2+ and Co2+ content in clinical laboratories, food industry and environmental control service.

ce:section.ce:para: The importance of monitoring ions of heavy and transitional metals spans all areas of science and technology. A system that can provide selective detection of these ions in aqueous solutions will find numerous applications in such fields as environmental monitoring, biomedical research, clinical chemistry and pharmacology. Ions of heavy and transitional metals in trace quantities are essential mineral components for the human body and all other living organisms, but they exhibit the high level of toxicity at higher concentrations [1–2].

Manganese (Mn) and cobalt (Co) are considered as the most abundant trace elements in the biosphere, being widely distributed in soil, sediment, water and live organisms [3–6]. In human, chronic manganese excess affects the central nervous system, with the symptoms resembling those of Parkinson's disease and autism [7–11]. The mean total content of Mn in human adult is about 15mg (typically seen in nucleic acids), and the requirement of in this element is about 2–5mg/day [6]. Mn acts as an activator of enzymes and as a component of metalloenzymes, taking part in oxidative phosphorylation, fatty acids and cholesterol metabolism, mucopolysaccharide metabolism, and urea cycle [6].

Co is an essential element for life in minute amounts [5]. The human adult contains about 1.1mg Co with the daily requirement of 0.1μg/day. Co is a component of vitamin B12. Co induces erythropoietin and blocks iodine uptake by the thyroid, controls the transfer of enzymes like homocysteine methyltransferase in methionine metabolism [6]. Contact dermatitis, cardiomyopathy, vision or hearing impairment, hypothyroidism and polycythemia, have been attributed to chronic of cobalt ions due to dietary supplements, occupation and medical devices (Co-containing implants). The knowledge of steady state Co concentrations in tissues in combination with the data on its adverse health effects in humans should help in the characterization of potential hazards associated with increased blood Co concentrations due to exposure to dietary supplements or cobalt‑chromium (Co-Cr) containing implants [12].

Thus, the possibility to monitor Mn2+ and Co2+ ions can represent a helpful tool for clinical diagnostics, food industry and environmental control service. The analysis of the localization and distribution of essential and beneficial metals (including Mn and Co) in biological tissues and liquids (e.g. blood and urine) is a challenging task for medicine and other life sciences. Blood concentrations as well as urinary excretion rates of metallic ions are reliable biomarkers for systemic exposure to these compounds [1,7,13].

Many instrument-based techniques have been applied for quantitative analysis of metal ions. Analytical methods such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), gas chromatography, atomic absorption and emission spectroscopy, mass spectrometry (MS) are currently and widely used [14–15]. The classical approaches of spectrophotometry and fluorometry, especially in combination with micro- and nanotechnologies [4,16–19] are very popular too.

Over the past years, the development and application of different MS imaging techniques for metals' analysis, including laser ablation inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (LA-ICP-MS), has been rapidly growing in the life sciences in order to investigate the uptake and the transport of both essential and toxic metals in cells and tissues [20]. Although the high-tech analytical techniques for detection of heavy and transition metals are highly sensitive and accurate, they are, however, time-consuming, expensive, require special skills and show limited capability for real-time measurements in biological samples.

As an attractive choice to the mentioned above methods, the electrochemical analytical approaches are also extensively used for metal ions assay. Highly specific, sensitive, simple, fast and cheap biosensors based on electrochemical transducers (amperometric and conductometric electrodes, potentiometric pH-sensitive field effect transistors) and different biorecognition molecules as sensitive elements were developed for inhibitory analysis of heavy metals ions [21–22]. Different microbial cells [23–24], enzymes [22,25–27], DNA and antibodies [28–33], as well as biomimics (molecularly imprinted polymers) [22,34] were used as bioelements. These analytical devices are promising tools for needs of health care, environmental control, biotechnology, agriculture and food industries. Improvement of their analytical characteristics (e.g. sensitivity, selectivity, stability etc.) may be achieved by using negatively or positively charged additional semi-permeable membranes, micro- and nanomaterials of different origin, genetically modified enzymes [22].

Being rather simple, inexpensive, and portable, bio- and chemosensors are promising for real-time measuring for online and continuous analysis of natural samples for detection of metallic ions. The number of chemosensors, namely, based on platinum nanoparticles and 3,3,5,5-tetramethylbenzidine as chromogene with fiber optic detection [35] as well as based on gold clusters [36] and fluorescence graphene-based quantum dots [37–39] were proposed in recent years.

Thus, a majority of known chemical and physico-chemical methods of metallic ions assay has a number of disadvantages, such as a low sensitivity and selectivity, high costs and complexity of the equipment. Therefore, the development of simple cost-effective sensitive methods of quantitative analysis of metallic ions, including Mn2+ and Co2+, is an important task of analytical chemistry. Thus, metal-ion-dependent enzymes [22,25–27] and catalytic nucleic acids – DNA-zymes or RNA-zymes [28–33,40] present promising tools for elaboration of such methods.

The use of functional nucleic acids as bioelements for the lab-on-a-chip (LOC) biosensors for detection of heavy metal ions have made a great step forward in recent years. A number of metal-ion-dependent DNAzymes and metal-ion-binding DNA structures have been obtained through combinatorial selection and rational design [33]. These molecules have been used as bioselective elements of sensors with fluorescent, colorimetric, electrochemical, and surface Raman detection of correspondent metal ions. For selective sensing metal ions in complex biological samples and live cells, a facile and stable biosensor based on non-biological enantiomer (L-DNAzyme) was proposed by Kue et al. [32]. With its highly sensitivity (with a detection limit down to 11ppt) and selectivity (up to millions-fold) toward specific metal ions, these sensors have been applied for on-site and real-time environmental monitoring, point-of-care medical diagnostics and for in situ cellular imaging [41].

According to the degree of automation and system integration, modern analytical devices may be classified as: microfluidic LOC system, microchip, lateral flow dipstick, personal glucose meter, disc-based analytical platform and microfluidic paper-based (MFPB) sensors [42–44]. MFPB sensors are inexpensive, simple, low-cost, portable, easy to use and usually naked-eye quantitative methods, so these analytical devices are promising for use in developing countries and for field measurements.

Analysis of literature data suggests that the further development of novel highly selective and sensitive methods for clinical diagnostics and industry, including enzymatic ones, is necessary. Recently we have demonstrated the possibility to use apoenzyme of Mn2+-dependent recombinant human liver arginase I as a Mn2+-sensitive bioelement in biosensor analysis [45]. The bi-enzyme amperometric biosensor based on arginase, isolated from the recombinant yeast cells, and commercial urease revealed a high sensitivity to Mn2+-ions (9200±20AM−1 m−2) and a low detection limit (0.15μM).

Arginase I (EC 3.5.3.1; L-arginine amidinohydrolase) is an Mn2+ containing enzyme of the urea cycle. It catalyses the final cytosolic reaction of urea formation in the mammalian liver — the conversion of arginine to ornithine and urea. Arginase I (further – arginase) has recently been considered as a prospective pharmaceutical in enzymotherapy for some kinds of auxotrophic cancers for L-arginine (further - Arg) as well as an analytical instrument for assay of the own substrate — Arg [46].

In this paper, we describe the development of a novel enzymo-chemical method for differential Mn2+ and Co2+ ions determination and its application on the real samples of wastewaters. The proposed method, based on apoenzyme of recombinant arginase, revealed a high sensitivity and selectivity to Mn2+ and Co2+ ions. The binding of Mn2+ or Co2+ with apoenzyme of arginase, at experimentally estimated optimal pH values, induces holoenzyme reconstruction, followed by recovering arginase activity and generation of urea from Arg in arginase-catalysed reaction at different pH-values: 9,1 and 7,1 for Mn2+ and Co2+, respectively. Analytical signal is the result of the formation of a stable product between urea and 2,3-butanedione monoxime (DMO) in an acidic medium [47]. The resulted compound can be spectrophotometrically and fluorometrically monitored. The advantages of the proposed method are the simplicity and the fastness (if compared to HPLC and other instrumental methods) of analytic procedure. Being highly sensitive, selective, valid and cost-effective, the proposed analytical method will be promising for Mn2+ and Co2+ ions assay in different fields of science and technology, including environmental chemistry, plant and animal biochemistry, nutrition, and medicine.

2,3-butanedione monoxime (DMO), disodium salt of ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), Ni-NTA Superflow beads and sulfuric acid (95–98%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. L-arginine (Arg) and inorganic salts were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).
Chemical reagents were prepared using Millipore milli-Q water pretreated with sorbent Chelex 100 (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA) to remove traces of transient metal ions.

The fluorescence of the end product of analytical reactions was measured using a Tecan Infinite M-200 spectrofluorometer (Thermo Scientific, United States) at an excitation wavelength of 360nm and an emission wavelength of 515nm. Optical density was measured at 350nm using a Shimadzu UV1650 PC spectrophotometer (Japan).

Recombinant human arginase I (further – arginase) has been expressed in Saccharomyces cerevisiae yeast cells under the control of the inducible CUP1 promoter. Isolation and purification of (His)6-tagged arginase from cell-free extract of cultivated cells using affinity chromatography on Ni-NTA sorbent was performed as described by us earlier [48]. Arginase activity was determined as the rate of urea production (μmolemin−1 mg−1 of protein), using as substrate solution of 100mM Arg, 2mM MnCl2 in 20mM Tris-sulfate buffer, pH9.5 [49].

The apo-arginase was prepared by dialysis of the purified arginase for 48h against several changes of 50mM sodium-phosphate buffer (PB), at pH8.0, containing 50mM NaCl and 20mM EDTA. EDTA was removed from the resulting apo-enzyme solution by three steps of dialysis against PB, containing 1M NaCl and Chelex (0.05gmL−1), and finally two dialysis against PB with 50mM NaCl and Chelex (0.05gmL−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration Graphs for Manganese (II) and Cobalt (II) Ions Assay
ce:section.ce:para:
Calibration procedure was as follows:
a)
Holoenzyme reconstruction: 0.02mL standard MnCl2 or CoCl2 solution was mixed with 0.02mL apo-arginase (900UmL−1) in TB, pH7.1 and incubated at 55°C during 10min;
b)
Enzymatic Arg hydrolysis: 0.2mL Arg solution was added to the mixture and incubated at 37°C during 20min;
c)
Generated urea assay: 3mL of chemical reagent was added and the mixture was heated in a boiled-water bath during 50min.

The product of chemical reaction of urea with DMO was detected by fluorometry (DMO-F) or spectrophotometry (DMO-S) as described earlier [50]. As a control, a reaction mixture without Arg was used. Each experiment was repeated 3 times. Calibration graphs for metal ions assay were built as the dependence of the absorbance (D490) or fluorescence intensity (I, FIU) of reaction product on the concentrations of metallic ion in final reaction mixture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal Ions Assay in Real Samples
ce:section.ce:para: Both the apo-arginase/DMO-F method and the reference atomic absorption method were then used to analyse real samples of wastewater from the copper and gold mines (Poland).

Before the enzymatic assay, all samples of wastewater were step-wise diluted in 8, 16, 32 and 64-folds with the 30mM TB: for Mn2+ assay – at pH9.1 and for Co2+ assay – at pH7.1. All samples were then analyzed using a standard addition test (SAT). 20μL of sample or standard solutions were added into the plastic tubes, then 10μL apo-arginase (1.2UmL−1) and 10μL 30mM TB, pH7.1 at 55°C for reconstruction of holo-arginase from the apo-arginase. Subsequently, 0.2mL Arg solution was added and the mixture was incubated at 37°C during 20min. The resulted urea was detected by adding 3mL DMO reagent. Thus, the added standards were diluted in final reaction mixture in 162 folds. As a control, reaction mixture without Arg was used. Each assay was performed for two dilutions of the sample and repeated 3 times.

In order to compare the results obtained by current method, the samples were also analyzed using a reference method which involved an Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometer (AAS–1N, Carl Zeiss Jena, Germany) with standard hallow cathode lamps and air-propane-butane flame. The analysis was carried out at 279.5nm (for Mn2+) and at 240.7nm (for Co2+) using a 1000ppm certified atomic absorption standard solutions (Fisher Scientific, Dubuque, IA, USA) for calibration. The analytical results were statistically processed using the OriginPro 8.5 software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Study of Optimal Conditions for Reconstitution of Arginase Holoenzyme and Enzymatic Hydrolysis of Arg
ce:section.ce:para: Arginase catalyses the final cytosolic reaction of urea cycle in the mammalian liver – the conversion of Arg into L-ornithine and urea. Arginase was discovered more than a century ago, a lot of scientific papers (>500,000) were published concerning this enzyme till today. The numbers of native enzymes, isolated from livers, erythrocytes, kidneys of different mammalians, plants, micro- and other organisms, as well as recombinant arginase preparations, were shown to be manganese-containing proteins. One of the first experiments on arginase activation with metallic ions, including Mn2+, was carried out in 1935 [51].
In this paper, we suggest to assay manganese/cobalt ions by determination of arginine-hydrolyzing activity after specific binding of the target ions with apoarginase (Fig. 1 ).

The possibility of replacing two Mn2+ ions, that usually present in human arginase, by Co2+ was recently approved [52]. The modified enzyme has been shown to possess a significantly lowered KM value without a concomitant reduction in kcat. The replacement of Mn2+ by Co2+ resulted in essential shift of the pH-optimum for the enzyme from 8.5 to 7.5. The modified enzyme is very promising for therapeutic applications, because Co2+ substitution leads to a significant increasing of the enzyme stability and activity in serum (neutral pH) [52]. Replacing Mn ion in active site of arginase by Co was assumed by us for quantitative detection of Co2+ after reconstruction of Co2+-modified enzyme and measuring the enzymatic activity at pH7.1, while Mn2+-containing enzyme shows its major activity at pH≥8.5 (we used pH9.1).

A schematic representation of the reactions, exploited for Mn2+/Co2+ assay, is shown in Fig. 1. The apo-arginase could recover its enzyme activity by the addition of Mn or Co ions. Binding of Mn2+ or Co2+ with apoenzyme resulted in reconstruction of two forms of holoenzyme (stage 1); Arg hydrolysis at pH9.1 and 7.1 (stage 2). Analytical signal was generated due to the formation of a stable product between urea and DMO (stage 3, chemical reaction). The last product is monitored spectrophotometrically (DMO-S) or fluorometrically (DMO-F). Thus, the concentrations of metal ions in samples were determined by measuring of the rate of urea formation during enzymatic hydrolysis of Arg under the action of reconstructed holoarginase.

We have demonstrated the possibility of reconstruction of apo-arginase into active holo-arginase in the presence of manganese ions (ranging from 0.1 to 1μM). The optimal time for the whole reconstruction of the holoenzyme was shown to be about 3min that highly correlates with the results of enzyme reconstruction on the surface of working electrode in amperometric biosensor [45].

The recovered Mn2+-dependent arginase activity was found to be proportional to the reaction time. The restored arginase activity reached a plateau (about 70% of initial activity) after 3min incubation. It is assumed that 30% of the original activity was not recovered due to slight denaturation of apo-arginase during its isolation. To optimize the conditions of the enzymatic reaction, the concentration of apo-arginase was varied from 0.003 to 0.015μgmL−1 in the incubation mixture. As a result, the optimal apo-arginase concentration in the incubation mixture has been estimated to be 0.012μgmL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Analytical Characteristics of Apo-Arginase-Based Method
ce:section.ce:para: The Mn2+ and Co2+ dependent holo-arginase were reconstructed by adding optimal amounts of Mn2+ and Co2+. Enzymatic activity was then monitored after addition of Arg using fluorometric or spectrophotometric registration of the final product of enzymatic digestion, urea.

The dependence of fluorescent intensity and optical density of the resulted product (DMO-urea) on Mn2+ and Co2+ concentration, as well as the corresponding calibration curves (DMO-F-and DMO-S) for Mn2+ and Co2+ assay performed using the optimized experimental conditions are shown in Fig. 2 . The optimal concentrations of the metal ions for maximal enzyme activity in the final reaction mixture was in the range 1pM to 1.4μM.

The analytical characteristics of apo-arginase-DMO-based methods for manganese and cobalt ions assay are summarized in Table 1 . Analytical signals of the developed method were tested in the range of concentrations from 1pM to 1.4μM for Co2+ and Mn2+ (Fig. 2). In the case of DMO-F method, the dynamic range is linear between 4pM and 1,1nM for Mn2+ assay and between 8pM and 45nM for Co2+ assay. For DMO-S method, the linearity ranges are 15pM–2.1nM for Mn2+ assay and 13pM−2.1nM for Co2+ assay.
For DMO-S and DMO-F modes of detection, the LODs for Mn2+ assay are 6pM and 1pM, and for Co2+ assay - 6pM and 2.5pM, respectively (Table 1).

The reconstruction time for complete reactivation of arginase holoenzyme was shown to be 10min. So, the obtained results clearly prove a possibility to use the apoenzyme of recombinant arginase I in enzymatic-chemical method of Mn2+ and Co2+ assay, including the analysis of these ions in real samples of wastewaters.

The high selectivity is a necessary demand for each analytical method. To study the selectivity of the developed apo-arginase-DMO-based approach, fluorescence and spectrophotometric measurements with various metal ions (Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Au3+) at two concentrations (15 and 30μM) were performed. The analytical signal on Mn2+-ions was taken as 100% (Fig. 3 ). For holoenzyme reconstruction, the apo-arginase was incubated with 30μM metal ions during 10min at 55°C. This temperature was chosen for reconstruction stage according to our observations [45,53] and literature data [54]. The reconstructed enzyme was incubated in the presence of 1mM Arg and urea content was estimated with DMO.

The negative signals for other tested metal ions, which usually present in wastewaters from mines, confirmed the high selectivity of apo-arginase to its natural (Mn2+) and artificial (Co2+) cofactors. This is due to high binding capacity of apo-enzyme to manganese and cobalt ions and very essential difference in pH-optima for Mn- and Co-bound holoenzymes – 9.1 and 7.1, respectively (Fig. 3). Generally, a very high selectivity of arginase apo-enzyme to metal ions is explained by selection of unique structure of catalytic center of the enzyme during hundreds millions years of biological evolution.

Thus, the developed apo-arginase-based methods (DMO-S and DMO-F) for the quantitative Mn2+ and Co2+ assay are very sensitive (due to high catalytic activity of the reconstructed arginase) and highly selective (see Table 1 and Fig. 3). It is worth mentioning that, due a very high catalytic activity of the holoenzyme, only 0.072ng of pure apo-arginase is required for each assay (see Materials and Methods). Taking into account that the final yield of arginase protein from recombinant yeast cells is approximately 6mgL−1 [48], 30×106 tests (with 3-fold repeating) can be done with the enzyme preparation, isolated from 1L of recombinant yeast culture. Storage stabilities (under −20°C) of holo-enzyme as well as of apo-enzyme are >3years, so we can conclude that the proposed method, being not simple, unfortunately, in the same time is very economic in costs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of Mn2+ and Co2+ in Real Wastewater Samples
ce:section.ce:para: Wastewaters from mines usually contain ions of heavy metals with a predominance of gold and copper. The developed apo-arginase-DMO method was tested on real samples of wastewater from copper and gold mines. To estimate the content of Mn and Co ions, apo-arginase was incubated with samples of wastewater followed by the addition of Arg (see section “Metal ions assay in real samples”) and fluorometric detection of produced urea with DMO.

Due to an essential impact of real samples matrix on analytical signal to the target analytes, a standard addition test (SAT) was performed. SAT is a type of approach for quantitative analysis often used in analytical chemistry, whereby the standard is added directly to the aliquots of the analyzed sample. SAT is used in situations where sample components also may contribute to the analytical signal, thus making it impossible to use routine calibration method. The results of estimation of Mn2+ and Co2+ in wastewaters from gold and copper mines are presented in Fig. 4 and Table 2 .

The reproducibility of the proposed analytical methods is satisfactory: the coefficients of variation are <6.7%. It is worthwhile mentioning that Mn2+ and Co2+ contents, which were obtained by us, are close to ranges published by another authors: 18.7–110μM for Mn2+ in coal-mine wastewater [55] and 30–310μM for Co2+ in copper and cobalt-mine wastewater [56].

The values of Mn2+ and Co2+ content in the analyzed samples (Table 2), obtained by two methods – the new enzymatic approach and the reference atomic absorption method - demonstrated a strong correlation: R =0.995 (R =0.995, 95% confidence interval, p <0.01) (Fig. 5 ).

Thus, the developed apo-arginase-DMO-F enzymatic method can be used for monitoring Mn2+ and Co2+ in the samples of wastewaters. On the other hand, this method being very sensitive could be useful to test implant patients for signs of hypothyroidism and polycythemia starting at blood or serum Co concentrations above 100μgL−1 [57]. Besides it, the proposed apo-enzyme-based analytical approach will be useful for fundamental investigations: to study in detail in vitro interactions between metal-dependent biomolecules (proteins and DNA) and their metal cofactors [58].

The developed analytical method was evaluated using statistical analysis. The Student's t-test was applied and does not reveal significant difference between the experimental values obtained in the sample analysis by the two methods. The calculated t-value (tcalc =2.64) in the case of Mn2+ assay in gold mine was found to be less than the critical t-value (tcrit =4.3) at 5% significance level. Similar data were obtained for Mn2+ assay in wastewater of copper mine: the calculated t-value (tcalc =2.33) was found to be less than the critical t-value (tcrit =4.3) at 5% significance level. The calculated t-value (tcalc =0.76) for Co2+ assay in gold mine was found to be less than the critical t-value (tcrit =4.3) as well as the calculated t-value (tcalc =1.11) for Co2+ assay in copper mine was found to be less than the critical t-value (tcrit =4.3) at 5% significance level. The assessment of the validation parameters of the method is given in Table 3 .
Thus, the results of the accuracy research of the method of Mn2 and Co2+ determination was shown to meet requirements for the permissible limits of ±10.0%.

The advantages of the novel enzymo-chemical method for Mn2+ and Co2+ assay are simplicity of analytical procedure and economic effect due to the usage of only one enzyme in the form of apo-enzyme. An effectiveness of the apo-arginase-based analytical method was proved on the real samples of wastewater. The obtained results proved to be in a good correlation with atomic absorption method. Due to a high stability of apo-arginase, the development and manufacture of enzymatic kits for metallic ions assay based on the proposed method is very prospective. Being sensitive, valid, low-cost, highly selective, the proposed analytical method would be promising in different fields of fundamental and practical science, including environmental chemistry, plant and animal biochemistry, nutrition, and medicine.
This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
The authors declare that there is no conflict of interests regarding the publication of this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Academy of Sciences (NAS) of Ukraine (program “Sensors for Medical, Environmental, Industrial, and Technological Needs: Metrological Support and Trial Operation”, Project 13-2017), by the Ministry of Education and Science of Ukraine (project #0116U004737), by NATO (Project CBP. NUKR.SFP 984173) and by fellowship of President of Ukraine for Young Scientists in 2017 (N. Stasyuk). We acknowledge the funding through the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and Programme entitled HEARTEN under grant agreement No 643694.
